text:
The most overrated kind of beauty, in my view, is the kind that announces itself immediately and demands to be recognized. Symmetrical, polished, obviously composed beauty that leaves no work for the eye or the mind. A face that photographs perfectly, a landscape that looks like a screensaver, a building that wins awards the moment it opens. There is nothing wrong with it exactly, but there is something exhausting about beauty that is so complete it shuts the conversation down rather than opening it.

This is the beauty of the first glance that never deepens. You see it once and you have essentially seen it fully. It does not accumulate meaning over time or reward returning attention. It tends to age poorly, not physically but experientially, because so much of its power came from the initial impact and there is nothing underneath to sustain interest.

Compare that to beauty that is slightly off, that has a flaw or an asymmetry or an unconventional quality that initially gives you pause and then slowly reorganizes your sense of what is possible. A face with some strangeness to it. A piece of music that does not resolve the way you expected. A room that seems almost wrong until one day it seems inevitable. That kind of beauty earns your attention over time rather than demanding it upfront.

The overrated kind is also heavily socially validated, which adds a strange social pressure to the experience. You feel you are supposed to find it beautiful, that everyone agrees it is beautiful, and that any reservation you have reflects a failure in yourself. That consensus flattens the experience. Real aesthetic response is personal and a little eccentric, and beauty that everyone agrees upon often ends up being more about agreement than about genuine experience.

So my answer is: conventional, legible, immediately striking beauty. It is celebrated far beyond what it actually delivers.
